Question: The minimal stimulus needed to cause a neuron to transmit an impulse is called the ________
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Explain graded potentials and threshold stimulus.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Threshold potential (typically around -55 mV) is the minimum membrane depolarization required to open voltage-gated sodium channels and trigger an action potential.

Question: A neuron with many processes one of which is an axon is classified structurally as ________
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Classify neurons structurally as multipolar bipolar or unipolar.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Multipolar neurons have multiple dendrites and a single axon extending from the cell body representing the most common neuron type in the CNS.

Question: Just before an action potential is generated which statement is correct regarding the movement of ions?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Describe ionic movements during action potential initiation.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Opening of voltage-gated sodium channels allows rapid inward diffusion of Na+ ions depolarizing the axonal membrane toward threshold.

Question: What is the role of calcium ions in the pre-synaptic knob?
Option A: Synaptic vesicles fuse to knob membrane
Option C: Neurotransmitters move back into pre-synaptic neuron
Correct Answer: Synaptic vesicles fuse to knob membrane
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Describe presynaptic events during synaptic transmission.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Calcium influx into the presynaptic terminal triggers exocytosis causing synaptic vesicles to fuse with the presynaptic membrane and release neurotransmitters.

Question: What is it called when a neuron is polarized further decreasing the likelihood that an action potential will be generated?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Inhibition occurs when hyperpolarizing IPSPs move the postsynaptic membrane potential further below threshold making action potential generation less likely.

Question: Depolarization of the cell membrane occurs when there is a rapid influx (inflow) of
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Explain membrane depolarization during action potentials.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Rapid influx of positively charged sodium (Na+) ions through open voltage-gated channels reverses membrane polarity from negative to positive.

Question: The dendrite and cell body of a neuron contain which of the following proteins?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Identify membrane proteins of neuronal dendrites and cell bodies.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: The somatodendritic membrane houses chemically-gated neurotransmitter receptors ion channels and Na+/K+ pumps to process incoming postsynaptic signals.

Question: Which neuroglial cells form the insulating myelin sheaths around axons located within the central nervous system (CNS)?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Identify types and functions of neuroglia in the CNS and PNS.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Oligodendrocytes produce myelin sheaths around central nervous system axons whereas Schwann cells myelinate axons in the peripheral nervous system.

Question: Star-shaped neuroglial cells in the CNS that support neurons regulate interstitial fluid and form the blood-brain barrier are called
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Describe functions of astrocytes in maintaining the neural microenvironment.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Astrocytes provide structural support regulate ion/neurotransmitter concentrations and form perivascular feet constructing the blood-brain barrier.

Question: The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is approximately -70 mV which is primarily maintained by the active transport of
Option A: 3 Na+ out of the cell for every 2 K+ into the cell
Option B: 2 Na+ out of the cell for every 3 K+ into the cell
Option C: 3 K+ out of the cell for every 2 Na+ into the cell
Option D: 2 Ca2+ out of the cell for every 1 Na+ into the cell
Correct Answer: 3 Na+ out of the cell for every 2 K+ into the cell
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Explain the ionic basis and transport mechanisms maintaining resting membrane potential.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: The Na+/K+ ATPase pump actively transports 3 Na+ out of the cell for every 2 K+ into the cell per ATP hydrolyzed.

Question: During the repolarization phase of an action potential the membrane potential returns toward resting potential due to the opening of voltage-gated
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Describe ion flux events during action potential depolarization and repolarization.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Repolarization occurs when voltage-gated K+ channels open allowing positive potassium ions to diffuse out of the axon.

Question: Action potentials travel significantly faster along myelinated axons because impulse conduction jumps from node to node a process called
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Distinguish between continuous and saltatory action potential propagation.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Saltatory conduction is the rapid leaping of action potentials from one node of Ranvier to another in myelinated axons.

Question: The cone-shaped region of the neuron cell body where the axon originates and where action potentials are generated (trigger zone) is the
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Identify anatomical regions of a neuron.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: The axon hillock acts as the trigger zone where graded potentials summarize to determine if threshold is reached to initiate an action potential.

Question: The period during and immediately following an action potential during which another threshold stimulus cannot trigger a second action potential is called the
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Define absolute and relative refractory periods.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: Inactivation of voltage-gated Na+ channels during the absolute refractory period prevents any subsequent action potential generation.

Question: When a neurotransmitter opens chemically-gated sodium channels on the postsynaptic membrane causing a localized depolarization this response is called an
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Distinguish between EPSP and IPSP graded potentials.
Chapter: Chapter 10: Nervous System I – Basic Structure and Function
Explanation: An EPSP is a localized graded depolarization of the postsynaptic membrane produced by Na+ influx driving the membrane potential closer to threshold.
